scaffolds.q: Your voice comes from vibrations in your throat.
scaffolds.explain: Air passing through your larynx vibrates vocal cords.

scaffolds.q: Swallowing moves food from mouth to stomach.
scaffolds.explain: Throat muscles coordinate to push food down.

scaffolds.q: A hoarse voice means something is wrong with the voice box.
scaffolds.explain: If vocal cords cannot move properly, voice sounds hoarse.

scaffolds.q: The vagus nerve (CN X) controls muscles in the throat.
scaffolds.explain: CN X innervates larynx and pharynx muscles.

scaffolds.q: If swallowing muscles are paralyzed, food can go down wrong.
scaffolds.explain: Food might enter airway instead of esophagus.

scaffolds.q: CN X is the vagus nerve.
scaffolds.explain: It controls throat muscles for voice and swallowing.

scaffolds.q: Voice and swallowing problems together suggest one nerve controls both.
scaffolds.explain: When two functions fail together, look for one nerve.

scaffolds.q: The vagus nerve innervates larynx and pharynx muscles.
scaffolds.explain: CN X controls voice and swallowing.

scaffolds.q: Vagal damage can cause aspiration from impaired swallowing.
scaffolds.explain: Without proper swallowing, food/liquid can enter airway.
